coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as efficient turn-on fluorescent probe for assay of organophosphorus pesticides
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Publishing Services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Publishing Services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jiqing
Chu, Shushu
Tao, Chenyu
Yan, Jinghao
Jiang, Yuanyuan
Lu, Yizhong
coredata.dc:description: Concerns regarding human health and food safety have generated interest in developing simple, accurate, and cost-effective strategies for evaluating organophosphorus pesticide (OP) residues. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized from a common low-cost precursor via a simple pyrolysis process. The fluorescence of the N-CDs can be suppressed by p-nitrophenol, which is the hydrolysis product of the p-nitrophenyl phosphate salt catalyzed by alkaline phosphatase (ALP). A fluorescent turn-on assay for the inhibitory effect of glyphosate was developed with a low detection limit and wide linear range. Moreover, the feasibility of visualizing OPs in vegetables was demonstrated by the fluorescence imaging of glyphosate on cabbage leaves. This research not only offers a facile method for the synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs but also inspires the development of effective multi-mode sensing platforms that include fluorescent testing and imaging for monitoring pesticide residues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Alkaline phosphatase
Organophosphorus pesticides
Logic gates
Fluorescence imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj subheadings tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs by p-NP
Determination of ALP activity by fluorescence assay

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Authors. Publishing Services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd.

National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22172063 and 21902062), Young Taishan Scholar Program (tsqn201812080), and Independent Cultivation Program of Innovation Team of Ji'nan City (2021GXRC052). In addition, we thank Prof. Hua Zhang of the University of Jinan for providing access to the equipment for fluorescence imaging using inverted fluorescence microscopy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) N-CD synthesis scheme, (b) TEM image, and (c) size distribution of N-CDs. (d) FTIR spectra of N-CDs and EDTA-2Na. (e) Survey XPS spectrum of the N-CDs. (f) 3D fluorescence spectrum of the N-CDs. (g) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra and UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N-CDs. The insets show photographs of the N-CDs under sunlight and UV light. (h) CIE color gamut coordinates of the N-CDs.

(a) Emission spectrum of N-CDs (excitation wavelength: 340 nm) and absorption spectrum of p-NP. (b) Fluorescence lifetimes of the N-CDs in the absence or presence of p-NP. (c) Detection mechanism of p-NP, ALP, and glyphosate based on fluorescent N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of p-NPP hydrolyzed by ALP. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of bare N-CDs, and N-CDs in the presence of p-NP, p-NPP, ALP, and p-NPP+ALP, respectively. (c) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in the presence of 200 µM p-NPP and different concentrations of ALP. (d) Relationship between the fluorescence emission intensity and ALP activity. (e) Histogram of fluorescence intensity for different inputs. (f) Truth table and (g) schematic of a “NAND” logic gate.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CD solution in the presence of various substances. (b) Fluorescence spectra of ALP+p-NPP+N-CDs after incorporation of glyphosate at different concentrations. (c) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity and glyphosate concentration. (d) Effects of various pesticides, ions, and sugars on this assay in the presence and absence of glyphosate. (e) Histogram of fluorescence intensity for different inputs. (f) Truth table and (g) schematic of tandem “Implication-NAND” logic gates.

(a) Schematic of the fluorescence imaging of glyphosate residue on cabbage leaf. The fluorescence imaging was carried out in blue channel: the cabbage leaf was added with N-CDs, ALP, p-NPP, and different concentrations of glyphosate (µg/mL): 0 (b), 2 (c), 5 (d), 10 (e) and 50 (f).
Results of detection of glyphosate concentration in some practical samples using the standard addition method.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as efficient turn-on fluorescent probe for assay of organophosphorus pesticides

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Concerns regarding human health and food safety have generated interest in developing simple, accurate, and cost-effective strategies for evaluating organophosphorus pesticide (OP) residues. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized from a common low-cost precursor via a simple pyrolysis process. The fluorescence of the N-CDs can be suppressed by p-nitrophenol, which is the hydrolysis product of the p-nitrophenyl phosphate salt catalyzed by alkaline phosphatase (ALP). A fluorescent turn-on assay for the inhibitory effect of glyphosate was developed with a low detection limit and wide linear range. Moreover, the feasibility of visualizing OPs in vegetables was demonstrated by the fluorescence imaging of glyphosate on cabbage leaves. This research not only offers a facile method for the synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs but also inspires the development of effective multi-mode sensing platforms that include fluorescent testing and imaging for monitoring pesticide residues.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are widely used and play crucial roles in insect control and crop growth regulation [1]. Glyphosate (N-(phosphonomethyl)glycine), an organophosphorus pesticide, is one of the most widely used commercial synthetic phosphonate herbicides [2]. However, excessive use of glyphosate can pollute the soil, air, water, and crop products [3,4]. Previous research has shown that OPs are suspected human carcinogens [5] and can lead to many diseases in humans, such as metabolic changes, Alzheimer's disease, kidney damage, Parkinson's syndrome, DNA damage, and reproductive toxicity [2,6,7]. Therefore, excessive glyphosate residue poses risks to the environment and food security, ultimately affecting human health. Therefore, it is important to develop accurate and sensitive methods for glyphosate detection.

The traditional methods for detecting OPs include gas chromatography (GC) [8], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [9], and electrochemical methods [10]. Although these traditional methods offer good selectivity and high sensitivity, they are complex, time-consuming, and require specialized staff to operate complex instruments. Considering these limitations, biosensors such as colorimetric [11] and fluorescent [12] assays have been developed for OP detection. Among these, fluorescent sensors have garnered significant attention because of their high sensitivity, rapid detection capability, and ease of operation. Zheng et al. developed a fluorescent sensor using CdTe QDs and acetylcholinesterase to detect organophosphates [13]. Additionally, Zhao et al. synthesized a fluorescent material based on (4-morpholino ethyl) boronic acid to assess organophosphorus pesticides by monitoring pH variations in the reaction with AChE and pesticides [14]. Most existing fluorescent sensors for detecting OPs utilize quantum dots, organic dyes, metal-organic frameworks, or composite materials as fluorescent probes [15–17]. The synthesis of these materials is often complex and costly, which significantly hinders their practical application. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a material with a simple synthesis method and a low cost that can serve as a fluorescent probe for OPs detection.

As a carbon-based nanomaterial with a size of less than 10 nm [18,19], carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention in catalysis, sterilization, fluorescence sensors, and cell imaging due to their large specific surface area, excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, and stable photoluminescence [20–26]. Recently, various fluorescence sensors based on CDs have been used to detect organophosphorus pesticides, including glyphosates. For example, a turn-on fluorescence probe for detecting glyphosate was developed based on the inhibition of acetylcholinesterase-choline oxidase to produce H2O2, and the fluorescence intensity of modification-free CDs was inhibited by the Fe3+ generated by the oxidation of Fe2+ by H2O2 [27]. Wang et al. designed a fluorescent probe (CDs/AgNPs) for the glyphosate assay because the fluorescence signals of CDs can be quenched by AgNPs, and glyphosate induces the aggregation of AgNPs and recovers the fluorescence [28]. However, these fluorescent detection methods for OPs are expensive and involve complex operations; some require the use of two or more enzymes, while others require the use of precious metals to quench the fluorescence of CDs. Moreover, their preparation methods include laser ablation [29,30], microwave-assisted pyrolysis [31,32], electrochemical oxidation [33,34], and hydrothermal synthesis [35]. These methods involve expensive instrumentation, harsh synthetic conditions, and complex separations. Therefore, a facile and low-cost CD preparation strategy as well as a simple and accurate detection assay for OPs are of great significance.

To address the aforementioned challenges, we synthesized highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots through the pyrolysis of a single raw material, disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na). The proposed synthesis strategy is simple and cost-effective. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of p-nitrophenol (p-NP) overlaps with the emission spectrum of the as-prepared N-CDs, leading to the inhibition of the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs by p-NP through the inner filter effect (IFE). Additionally, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) can hydrolyze p-nitrophenyl phosphate disodium salt (p-NPP) to form p-NP under alkaline conditions, and glyphosate can inhibit this hydrolysis by blocking the ALP activity. Consequently, an N-CD-based fluorescent turn-on sensor was designed for the detection of glyphosate. This sensor offers benefits such as low cost, simple operation, and high sensitivity and selectivity. Moreover, methods for detecting p-nitrophenol and ALP activity could be established using this process. Building on the proposed fluorescent sensor, a logic gate for the glyphosate assay was rationally developed, allowing for a convenient conversion of fluorescent signals into digital signals. Fluorescence imaging of glyphosate in cabbage leaves confirmed that this assay can be utilized for the fluorescent visualization of organophosphates (OPs) in practical samples such as fruits and vegetables.

Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) and ascorbic acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), lysine, and leucine were supplied by Shanghai MACKLIN Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glyphosate, acetamiprid (Ace), methylviologen (Met), dimethoate (Dim), 4-nitrophenyl phosphate disodium salt (p-NPP), p-nitrophenol(p-NP), glutathione, resorcinol, glucose, and fructose were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Uric acid was obtained from Saen Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glycine was purchased from Komio Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Thiamethoxam (Thi) was obtained from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All chemicals were purchased from commercial sources and used directly. All the water used in the experiment was deionized without any special treatment performed on it.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2100 Plus electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-8000 spectrophotometer (Shanghai METASH, China). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using an iS50 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The fluorescence spectra were recorded using an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ESCALAB Xi+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fluorescence imaging was performed using a Leica DMI8 fluorescence inverted microscope (Leica). A time-dependent single-photon counting spectrofluorometer system (Horiba, JY-IBH, Fluorocube-01) was used to obtain the photoluminescence decay curves of the samples.

The N-CDs were prepared via a simple sintering process using disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na) as a precursor. Specifically, 1.0 g of EDTA-2Na was heated in a N2 atmosphere for 2 h at 300 °C. The resulting calcined product was dispersed in 50 mL of methanol and stirred for 20 min, followed by centrifugation at 11000 r/min for 20 min to eliminate the insoluble sodium salt. Subsequently, the supernatant solution obtained after centrifugation was filtered through a microporous filter head (0.22 µm), and the N-CDs were finally obtained after drying.

N-CD solution (100 µL, 0.75 mg/mL) and p-NP at different concentrations were added to the Tris–HCl buffer solution (0.05 M, pH = 9.0). After mixing the solution for 1 min, fluorescence spectra were observed. The excitation wavelength was 340 nm.

To detect ALP, 10 µL of ALP at different concentrations was added to the buffer solution (Tris–HCl, pH = 9.0) containing 20 µL of p-NPP solution (10 mM). The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 5 min. After incubation, 100 µL of the N-CD solution was added to the mixture, and then the fluorescence spectra were measured.

Glyphosate solution (10 µL) at different concentrations was added to the buffer (Tris–HCl, pH = 9.0) containing 10 mU/mL ALP. The mixture solution was then incubated for half an hour at 37 °C. p-NPP solution (20 µL, 10 mM) was then added and incubated for 5 min at 37 °C. After incubation, 100 µL of the N-CD solution was added to the mixture, and then its fluorescence spectrum was measured. The anti-interference and selectivity experiments were conducted with and without glyphosate. The concentration of ions, sugars, and other biomolecules was 2 mM (Fe3+ concentration was 0.2 mM), and the concentration of other pesticides was 5 mg/mL.

Lake water was obtained from Jiazi Lake at the University of Jinan and filtered through a microporous filter membrane (0.22 µm). Tap water was obtained from the laboratory. Apples purchased from a local supermarket were soaked in deionized water for 30 min and quantitative water samples were collected. Various concentrations of glyphosate were added to the three water samples and the spiked real samples were tested.

To investigate the fluorescence imaging of the N-CDs in vegetables, cabbage leaves were cut and fixed on slides. Glyphosate at different concentrations (0, 2, 5, 10, and 50 µg/mL) was incubated with ALP for 30 min, after which p-NPP was added, and the cells were incubated for another 5 min. The incubated product was dropped onto the leaf, followed by the addition of the N-CD solution. Finally, leaves were observed using an inverted fluorescence microscope.

The N-CD sample was prepared by a straightforward sintering process using EDTA-2Na as both the carbon precursor and the nitrogen source (Fig. 1(a)). This method offers several advantages over other synthesis methods. For example, only one precursor is required in the synthesis process, making it cost-effective and easily obtainable. Additionally, the use of EDTA-2Na as the precursor surpasses commonly used natural raw materials in terms of reproducibility. The yield of the N-CDs obtained using the proposed method was 49.8%. The obtained N-CDs exhibited a higher fluorescence intensity than the CDs synthesized using EDTA-2Na as the precursor via the hydrothermal method (Fig. S1). The morphology of the N-CDs was characterized by TEM. As shown in Figs. 1(b)(c), the N-CDs demonstrated good monodispersity and a narrow size distribution (4.64 ± 1.44 nm), which is advantageous for subsequent sensing or imaging applications.

The structure and chemical composition of the designed N-CDs were characterized using Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The N-CDs showed five peaks located at 3439, 3028, 1593, 1406, and 1141 cm−1, which represented ν O–H, ν –CH2–, ν C = O, ν C = N, and ν C–O, respectively (Fig. 1(d)). Compared with EDTA-2Na, the –CH2– (3028 cm−1) antisymmetric tensile vibration in the N-CDs was reduced, indicating that the –CH2– species condensed into CDs after pyrolysis [36]. The abundant functional groups on the as-obtained N-CD surface contributed to their water dispersion and biocompatibility. XPS analysis was also conducted to determine the combination states of the elements. The XPS survey spectrum showed that C, N, and O were present in the N-CD sample (Fig. 1(e)). The C 1s spectrum (Fig. S2(a)) presented three peaks at 288.11, 285.93, and 284.82 eV, which corresponded to C=O, C–O/C–N, and C=C, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2(b), the XPS spectrum of O 1s could be divided into three peaks at 535.88, 532.22, and 531.00 eV, corresponding to the π-π* shake-up peak, C=O, and C–O, respectively [36]. In the N 1s spectrum (Fig. S2(c)), the peaks centered at 399.65 and 401.59 eV were assigned to pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [37]. The aforementioned characterizations prove that N-doped CDs with a small size, uniform dispersion, and abundant functional groups were successfully synthesized.

The 3D fluorescence emission spectrum of the N-CDs revealed that the emission wavelengths red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelengths, indicating excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior [38]. At the excitation wavelength of 330 nm, the strongest fluorescence emission was observed at 415 nm (Figs. 1(f) and S3). The inset in Fig. 1(g) shows that the N-CD dispersion appears yellow under daylight and emits blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). Additionally, the coordinates of the N-CDs in the CIE color domain, as shown in Fig. 1(h), were (0.1728, 0.1317), which is consistent with the fluorescence spectrum and luminous color of the N-CDs [39]. Furthermore, the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1g) displayed two peaks at 320 and 270 nm, attributed to the n–π* and π–π* transitions of the C=O group, respectively [36].

Photoluminescence stability is an important indicator of N-CD properties [39]. The fluorescence signals of the N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) initially increased and then stabilized as the pH increased from 5 to 12 (Fig. S4(a)). Upon continuous exposure to ultraviolet light (365 nm) for 60 min, the fluorescence signals of the N-CDs exhibited no significant changes, indicating excellent photostability of the synthesized N-CDs (Fig. S4(b)). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs remained consistent even in a high-salinity environment (0.5 M NaCl) (Fig. S4(c)). The quantum yield (QY) of the N-CDs excited at 330 nm was 5.95% (Fig. S5).

To illustrate the feasibility of detecting p-nitrophenol (p-NP) using N-CDs, p-NP was introduced into an N-CD dispersion. The fluorescence signals of the N-CDs decreased significantly upon the addition of p-NP (Fig. S6(a)). To determine the optimal conditions for detecting p-NP, parameters such as the pH of the buffer, incubation time between p-NP and N-CDs, and concentration of N-CDs were optimized. F1 and F0 denote the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of p-NP, respectively. The optimal conditions were set at a pH of 9.0, N-CD concentration of 0.75 mg/mL, and incubation time of 1 min (Figs. S6(b)–(d)).

The fluorescence signals of the N-CDs were recorded after introducing different amounts of p-NP under optimal conditions (Figs. S7(a)(b)). As the p-NP concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs gradually decreased. In the range of 0.1–30 and 30–200 µM, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs showed a good linear relationship with the p-NP concentration (Figs. S7(c)(d)). The limit of detection (LOD) was as low as 0.066 µΜ (S/N = 3). Compared to other detection methods for p-NP, the method using N-CDs had a lower LOD (Table S1). Fig. S8 shows that this method has high selectivity for detecting p-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs by p-NP
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the probable quenching mechanisms, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of p-NP was measured. The p-NP exhibited a strong absorption peak at 405 nm. In addition, there was a large overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of p-NP and the emission spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 2(a)). The fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs was 5.95 ns and remained almost unchanged when p-NP was added (Fig. 2(b)). Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N-CDs mixed with p-NP showed no significant changes compared to the absorption spectrum of p-NP (Fig. S9), demonstrating that no new ground-state complexes were formed between p-NP and the N-CDs. Thus, it can be reasonably inferred that the inner filter effect (IFE) is the mechanism by which p-NP inhibits the fluorescent signals of N-CDs [40–42]. In addition, the IFE effect can cause a redshift in the fluorescence of N-CDs [43]. From Fig. S6(a), the fluorescence signals of the N-CDs underwent a significant red shift with the addition of p-NP to the N-CD solution, revealing a close interaction between p-NP and N-CDs [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ALP activity by fluorescence assay
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the phenomenon that p-NP could distinctly quench the fluorescence signals of the N-CD dispersion, a fluorescence assay method was designed to measure ALP activity using p-NPP as the substrate. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), when ALP activity increased, the absorption peak (310 nm) of p-NPP decreased, and the absorption intensity (405 nm) of p-NP increased. These results indicate that p-NP was produced through the hydrolysis of p-NPP under the catalysis of ALP, and the p-NP concentration increased with an increase in ALP activity. The hydrolysis of p-NPP by ALP under alkaline conditions produced p-NP, which quenched the fluorescence signals of the N-CDs (Fig. 3(b)) and enabled the detection of alkaline phosphatase (Fig. 2(c)). In addition, the absorption peak of p-NPP (310 nm) partially overlapped with the optimal excitation wavelength of the N-CDs (330 nm), which caused weak quenching of the fluorescence of the N-CDs. Thus, an excitation wavelength of 340 nm was selected for the fluorescence detection of ALP activity because the interference of fluorescence quenching by p-NPP was much smaller at this excitation wavelength (Fig. S10). As ALP has the best catalytic activity at a pH of 9, Tris–HCl buffer (pH 9.0, 0.05 M) was selected as the buffer for incubation [41]. To fulfill the activity of ALP, 200 µM was set as the p-NPP concentration in further experiments. The incubation time and temperature for the ALP-hydrolyzed p-NPP reaction were also optimized (Fig. S11), and 5 min and 37 °C were set as the optimal conditions in the subsequent experiments.

ALP activity was measured under optimized detection conditions. Fig. 3(c) shows that as the ALP activity increased, the fluorescent signals of the N-CDs gradually decreased. Within the range of 0.1–10 mU/mL, a strong linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and ALP activity was observed (Fig. 3(d)). The LOD was calculated to be 0.05 mU/mL (S/N = 3). This N-CD-based fluorescence detection method for ALP activity showed a lower LOD compared to the other methods (Table S2). To demonstrate the selectivity and anti-interference performance of the proposed detection method, the effects of several common amino acids, ions, proteins, and enzymes on ALP detection were studied (Fig. S12). The results indicated that this method not only had good selectivity but also high anti-interference performance in the ALP assay, which was ascribed to the specificity of the enzyme-catalyzed reactions.

A binary NAND (not and) logic gate was constructed based on the proposed fluorescent assay for ALP activity. In this logic gate, p-NPP and ALP were used as inputs, and the maximum fluorescence intensity and luminescence color were used as outputs. When p-NPP or ALP were present, the input state was “1” , otherwise it was “0” . When the fluorescence intensity was greater than 40000, the output state was “1” , otherwise it was “0” . Regarding luminous color, when blue fluorescence was observed, the output state was “1”, otherwise it was “0”. As shown in Fig. 3(e), only when p-NPP and ALP were present simultaneously, that is, when the input state was (1,1), were the fluorescence signals of the N-CD solution inhibited and the output combination was 0. The histogram, truth table, and NAND logic gate diagrams are presented in Figs. 3(e)–(g), respectively.

Glyphosate, a typical organic phosphorus pesticide, effectively inhibits ALP activity, thereby preventing the formation of p-NP, which is the hydrolysis product of p-NPP by ALP. Therefore, this method can be applied to detect glyphosate (as illustrated in Fig. 2(c)). The control experiments shown in Fig. 4 (a) demonstrated the feasibility of the aforementioned strategy for OP detection. This detection method is influenced by factors such as ALP activity and the incubation time between ALP and glyphosate. As shown in Figs. S13(a)(b), after optimizing the experimental conditions, 10 mU/mL and 30 min were set as the ALP concentration and incubation time between ALP and glyphosate, respectively.

To demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed assay for detecting glyphosate, the response of the system to different glyphosate concentrations was recorded under optimized experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 4(b), as the glyphosate concentration increases from 0 to 40 µg/mL, the fluorescence emission intensity gradually increases. Fig. 4(c) shows that fluorescence intensity and glyphosate concentration display a satisfactory linear relationship within the ranges of 0.5–7 and 7–20 µg/mL. The LOD for glyphosate was calculated to be 0.026 µg/mL (3 N/S), which is close to or lower than that of the other methods (Table S3). Moreover, the LOD for glyphosate in the proposed method is far lower than the glyphosate limit of 0.9 mg/mL required by the World Health Organization.

The selectivity and anti-interference performance of the proposed method were examined by assessing the fluorescence intensity of the N-CD–p-NPP–ALP system towards metal cations, anions, sugars, and other pesticides, both in the absence and presence of glyphosate. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the N-CD–p-NPP–ALP system exhibited a distinct fluorescence response to glyphosate, leading to the restoration of N-CD fluorescence. Conversely, other ions, sugars, common biomolecules, and pesticides such as methylviologen (Met, an organochlorine pesticide), thiamethoxam, and acetamiprid (Thi and Ace, nicotine pesticides) did not affect the fluorescence of the system. These findings validate the exceptional selectivity of the N-CD–p-NPP–ALP system for glyphosate detection. Furthermore, when glyphosate and interfering substances were introduced simultaneously, the presence of the interfering substances affected the fluorescence intensity only minimally, indicating the good anti-interference capability of the sensor. In addition to glyphosate, the presence of other OPs, such as dimethoate (Dim), can also restore the inhibited fluorescence of N-CDs. This indicates that the sensor can be used for the detection of other organophosphorus pesticides, and a nanozyme sensor array should be designed for the discrimination and detection of multiple OPs.

Glyphosate was successfully detected using the N-CD–p-NPP–ALP system, which could further function as the implied logic gate equivalent to a combined logic gate in series with a previous NAND logic gate. In the first-layer “Implication” logic gate, glyphosate and ALP were taken as inputs. The input status was “1” when ALP or glyphosate were present and “0” otherwise. Then, the two inputs of the “Implication” logic gate were integrated into a second-level NAND logic gate to implement the combined “Implication-NAND” logic gate. When all three of p-NPP, ALP, and glyphosate were present at the same time and the input state was (1,1,1), the final logic gate output was “1”, indicating that glyphosate inhibited the hydrolysis of p-NPP under the catalysis of ALP and effectively restored the fluorescence of the N-CDs. The histogram, truth table, and “Implication-NAND” logic gate diagram are shown in Figs. 4(e)–(g), respectively.

The high anti-interference ability and selectivity of the N-CD–p-NPP–ALP system demonstrate its great potential for practical detection. Based on standard addition methods, glyphosate (0.5, 4, and 7 µg/mL) was added to the actual samples (lake water, tap water, and apple leaching solution), and the fluorescence was determined. The accuracy of the proposed method on the samples was evaluated by testing the recovery rate of glyphosate. As shown in Table 1 , the recovery rate of glyphosate in the spiked samples ranged between 90.2% and 109.8%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) remained within 3.3%. These results demonstrate that the N-CD–p-NPP–ALP system is feasible for the assay of glyphosate in real samples.

To study the fluorescence imaging ability of the proposed assay for the possible fluorescent visual detection of OPs, fluorescence imaging of glyphosate residues on cabbage leaves was performed. Fig. 5 (a) shows the specific steps of the fluorescence imaging procedure.

The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs was largely suppressed by the p-NP produced by the hydrolysis of p-NPP by ALP (Fig. 5(b)). With an increase in the glyphosate concentration, the blue fluorescence signals of the N-CDs were recovered, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity increased gradually (Figs. 5(c)–(f)). These results demonstrate that this method can be used as a universal visualization method for glyphosate residues in vegetables.

Highly fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized by sintering using a single precursor as the raw material. p-NP was found to quench the fluorescence of N-CDs via IFE. As ALP can hydrolyze p-NPP to produce p-NP and glyphosate can inhibit ALP activity, a new fluorescence turn-on detection method for glyphosate was established. The proposed method offers the advantages of a simple detection process, high sensitivity, good selectivity, and high anti-interference ability. The linear ranges for glyphosate detection were 0.5–7 and 7–20 µg/mL, and the limit of detection was found to be 0.026 µg/mL. This method can be easily applied to detect glyphosate in real-world samples. Fluorescence imaging of glyphosate residues on cabbage leaves showed that this method could be used for the visual detection of OP residues in vegetables. In addition, based on the above detection methods, an “Implication-NAND” logic gate, which can convert fluorescence signals into digital signals, was designed.

Jiqing Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Shushu Chu: Formal analysis, Data curation. Chenyu Tao: Visualization, Software. Jinghao Yan: Visualization, Resources. Yuanyuan Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yizhong Lu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22172063 and 21902062), Young Taishan Scholar Program (tsqn201812080), and Independent Cultivation Program of Innovation Team of Ji'nan City (2021GXRC052). In addition, we thank Prof. Hua Zhang of the University of Jinan for providing access to the equipment for fluorescence imaging using inverted fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chphma.2024.07.003.
